items.q: When do you use the imperfect rather than the passé composé?
items.correct: For ongoing, habitual, or descriptive past actions.
items.random_wrong: When you don't know what tense to use.
items.why: Students confuse past tenses by recency. The distinction is ASPECTUAL: passé composé = completed event ('I went'); imparfait = ongoing/habitual/descriptive ('I used to go', 'I was going').

items.q: What is the feminine plural form of 'beau'?
items.why: Students apply default endings to irregular adjectives. Beau has irregular forms: beau (m), belle (f), beaux (m pl), BELLES (f pl). The feminine takes belle, then adds -s.

items.q: How do you say 'I'm going to my friend's house' in French?
items.misconception: Je vais à la maison de mon ami.
items.why: Students translate word-for-word using 'à la maison de'. 'Chez' specifically means 'at the home/place of' someone: chez moi, chez le médecin, chez Pierre. Much more idiomatic and tested.

items.q: Who directed À bout de souffle (1960)?
items.why: It's Godard. “Truffaut” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
It's passé simple. “passé composé” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In what year was Les Quatre Cents Coups released?
items.why: The real figure is 1959. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
